perturbation.original_text: 1. DEFINITIONS AND INTE RPRETATION 1.1 The words defined below sh all have the following meaning: "Confidential Information" means all confidential information (however recorded, preserved or disclosed) disclosed or made available by the Discloser or any of its Representatives to the Recipient or any of its Represent atives including but not limited to: (a) any information that is marked, or at the time of disclosure is otherwise designated, as being confidential; (b) any information that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person on or relatin g to: (i) the business, affairs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans, intentions, or market opportunities of the Discloser; or (ii) the operations, processes, products, techniques and specifications, test procedures and results, inventions, know -how, tra de secrets, designs or software of the Discloser; (c) the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; (d) the existence and terms of this agreement; and (e) any inform ation or analysis derived from any of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. DEFINITIONS AND INTE RPRETATION 1.1 The words defined below sh all have the following meaning: "Confidential Information" means all information, whether or not explicitly marked or designated as confidential, that is disclosed or made available by the Discloser or any of its Representatives to the Recipient or any of its Represent atives including but not limited to: (a) any information that is marked, or at the time of disclosure is otherwise designated, as being confidential; (b) only information that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person on or relatin g to: (i) the business, affairs, customers, clients, suppliers, plans, intentions, or market opportunities of the Discloser; or (ii) the operations, processes, products, techniques and specifications, test procedures and results, inventions, know -how, tra de secrets, designs or software of the Discloser; (c) the fact that discussions and negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; (d) the existence and terms of this agreement; and (e) any inform ation or analysis derived from any of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This introduces a contradiction in the definition of 'Confidential Information'. Initially, it includes information 'marked, or at the time of disclosure is otherwise designated, as being confidential'. The modified version states 'all information, whether or not explicitly marked or designated as confidential,' but later maintains in point (b) that it is ONLY the information deemed confidential by a reasonable business person. This contradicts itself, making it unclear what information is actually protected under the agreement. This change causes in-text contradiction in clause 3. OBLIGATIONS OF CONFIDENTIALITY, specifically regarding what information needs to be kept confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. OBLIGATIONS OF CONFI DENTIALITY 3.1 The Recipient shall keep the Discloser's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Discloser or as expressly permitted by this agreement, shall: 3.1.1 not use the Confidential Information except for the Purpose; 3.1.2 not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party; 3.1.3 not make any copies, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Informa tion in whole or in part except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Discloser); 3.1.4 keep the Confidential Information in a safe and secure place and establish and main tain adequate security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use, including but not limited to implementing any reasonable security measures proposed by the Discloser from time to time; and 3.1.5 at the written requ est of the Discloser, or in any event on termination of this agreement, return or destroy all documents or other records containing Confidential Information to the Discloser and confirm to the Discloser that it has done so in writing. 3
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an in-text contradiction. Initially, the 'Confidential Information' definition broadly includes all information disclosed, 'whether or not explicitly marked'. However, it later restricts this to 'only information that would be regarded as confidential by a reasonable business person'. This directly conflicts with clause 3.1 of the 'OBLIGATIONS OF CONFIDENTIALITY,' which requires the recipient to keep the discloser's Confidential Information confidential. The change creates ambiguity regarding which information is actually considered 'Confidential Information', undermining the enforceability of the confidentiality obligations. This meets the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' because it provides conflicting obligations regarding the information that needs to be kept confidential.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 The Recipient may disclose all or part of the Discloser's Confidential Information to a Representative who needs to know that particular Confidential Information for the Pur pose provided that, in relation to each Representative to whom the Discloser's Confidential Information is to be disclosed, the Recipient prior to disclosure: 4.1.1 informs the Representative of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information; 4.1.2 informs the Discloser of the identity of the Representative to whom it intends to disclose Confidential Information; 4.1.3 procures that the Representative shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed, comply with this agreement as if it were the Recipient; and 4.1.4 if the Discloser so requests, in relation to a Representative other than an employee of the Recipient, procures that such Representative enters into a confidentiality agreement with the Discloser on terms equivalent to those contained in this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 The Recipient may disclose all or part of the Discloser's Confidential Information to a Representative who needs to know that particular Confidential Information for the Pur pose. The Recipient prior to disclosure: 4.1.1 informs the Representative of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information; 4.1.2 informs the Discloser of the identity of the Representative to whom it intends to disclose Confidential Information; 4.1.3 procures that the Representative shall, in relation to any Confidential Information disclosed, comply with this agreement as if it were the Recipient; and 4.1.4 if the Discloser so requests, in relation to a Representative other than an employee of the Recipient, procures that such Representative enters into a confidentiality agreement with the Discloser on terms equivalent to those contained in this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the conditional clause 'provided that, in relation to each Representative to whom the Discloser's Confidential Information is to be disclosed'. By removing this clause, it suggests that the subsequent requirements apply universally, regardless of the specific Representative or the 'need to know' principle, creating a direct contradiction to the original intent.This has an in-text contradiction with clause 2. COMPLIANCE WITH THIS AGREEMENT, as it's unclear now if the Recipient needs to comply with the terms of the agreement regarding who they share the Discloser's information with.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2. COMPLIANCE WITH THIS AGREEMENT In consideration of the Discloser or any of its Representatives disclosing or making available Confidential Information to the Recipient or any of its Representatives, the Recipient, on behalf of itself and its Representatives, undertakes to comply with the terms of this agreement. The Recipient shall, at all times, be liable for the failure of any of its Representatives to comply with the terms of this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the 'provided that' clause from section 4.1, which originally made the requirements in 4.1.1 - 4.1.4 conditional on the 'need to know' principle. By removing this condition, the obligations in 4.1.1 - 4.1.4 now apply universally, regardless of whether the Representative needs to know the information. This contradicts section 2, COMPLIANCE WITH THIS AGREEMENT, as it creates uncertainty about the Recipient's obligations regarding who they share the Discloser's information with, and whether those Representatives are required to comply with the agreement. This aligns with the definition of Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction, where different sections of a contract provide conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: 8. DEEMED DELIVERY 8.1 Any notice to be given in connection with this agreement shall be in wri ting in English and shall either be delivered by hand or sent by first class post or fax, email or other electronic form: 8.1.1 to any company which is a party at its registered office (or such other address as it may notify to the other parties to this ag reement for such purpose); or 8.1.2 to any individual who is a party at the address of that individual shown on page 1 of this agreement; (or in each such case such other address as the recipient may notify to the other parties for such purpose). 8.2 A co mmunication sent according clause 8.1 shall be deemed to have been received: 8.1.1 if delivered by hand, at the time of delivery ; 8.1.2 if sent by pre -paid first class post, on the second day after posting; or 8.1.3 if sent by fax, email or other electroni c form, at the time of completion of transmission by the sender.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. DEEMED DELIVERY 8.1 Any notice to be given in connection with this agreement shall be in wri ting in English and shall either be delivered by hand or sent by first class post or fax, email or other electronic form: 8.1.1 to any company which is a party at its registered office (or such other address as it may notify to the other parties to this ag reement for such purpose); or 8.1.2 to any individual who is a party at the address of that individual shown on page 1 of this agreement; (or in each such case such other address as the recipient may notify to the other parties for such purpose). 8.2 A co mmunication sent according clause 8.1 shall be deemed to have been received: 8.1.1 if delivered by hand, at the time of delivery ; 8.1.2 if sent by pre -paid first class post, on the third day after posting; or 8.1.3 if sent by fax, email or other electroni c form, at the time of receipt confirmation by the sender.
perturbation.explanation: This change modifies the deemed delivery times for notices. It alters the delivery time for first-class post from 'on the second day after posting' to 'on the third day after posting,' and email/fax communications from 'at the time of completion of transmission by the sender' to 'at the time of receipt confirmation by the sender.' This introduces a contradiction and causes ambiguity for both delivery methods.This change creates an in-text contradiction with clause 7. TERM AND TERMINATION, by creating uncertainty regarding the exact time of termination and when obligations come into effect.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. TERM AND TERMINATION If either party decides not to continue to be involved in the Purpose with the other party it shall notify the other party in writing and this agreement will terminate w ith immediate effect. The obligations of each party shall continue for a period of [ Insert number of years - this should be a realistic estimate of how long it is likely that the information that is to be disclosed will remain important to protect, e.g. 5 ] years from the termination of this agreement. Termination of this agreement shall not affect any accrued rights or remedies to which either party is entitled.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the 'DEEMED DELIVERY' terms, specifically altering the delivery time for first-class post and email/fax communications. The original text states first-class post is deemed received 'on the second day after posting', while the changed text states 'on the third day after posting'. For email and fax communications, the original text says deemed received 'at the time of completion of transmission by the sender', while the changed text states 'at the time of receipt confirmation by the sender'. This creates a direct contradiction with clause 7, 'TERM AND TERMINATION', because the timing of notice of termination is now uncertain, which creates a strong in-text contradiction within the document and directly aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition given.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 All Confidential Information shall remain the property of th e party disclosing it, which reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. No rights, including, but not limited to, any and all intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 All Confidential Information shall remain the property of th e party disclosing it, which reserves all rights in its Confidential Information. All rights, including, but not limited to, any and all intellectual property rights, in respect of a party's Confidential Information are granted to the other party for the duration of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction by reversing the statement about intellectual property rights. The original text explicitly states that 'No rights...are granted to the other party.' The altered text states 'All rights...are granted to the other party.' This direct contradiction introduces significant uncertainty regarding the ownership and usage rights of Confidential Information. Specifically, it has an in-text contradiction in clause 3. OBLIGATIONS OF CONFIDENTIALITY, regarding who has rights over the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. OBLIGATIONS OF CONFI DENTIALITY 3.1 The Recipient shall keep the Discloser's Confidential Information confidential and, except with the prior written consent of the Discloser or as expressly permitted by this agreement, shall: 3.1.1 not use the Confidential Information except for the Purpose; 3.1.2 not disclose or make available the Confidential Information in whole or in part to any third party; 3.1.3 not make any copies, reduce to writing or otherwise record the Confidential Informa tion in whole or in part except as strictly necessary for the Purpose (and any such copies, reductions to writing and records shall be the property of the Discloser); 3.1.4 keep the Confidential Information in a safe and secure place and establish and main tain adequate security measures to safeguard the Confidential Information from unauthorised access or use, including but not limited to implementing any reasonable security measures proposed by the Discloser from time to time; and 3.1.5 at the written requ est of the Discloser, or in any event on termination of this agreement, return or destroy all documents or other records containing Confidential Information to the Discloser and confirm to the Discloser that it has done so in writing. 3
perturbation.justification: The original text in 5.1 explicitly denies the granting of any intellectual property rights to the other party. The changed text in 5.1 directly contradicts this by stating that all rights, including intellectual property rights, are granted to the other party. This creates a direct conflict with clause 3, which outlines the Recipient's obligations of confidentiality and restrictions on the use and disclosure of the Discloser's Confidential Information. Specifically, 3.1.3 states that any copies or records of the Confidential Information 'shall be the property of the Discloser'. Granting all intellectual property rights to the other party directly contradicts the Discloser's ownership and control as defined in Clause 3, making the terms inconsistent and unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 9.6 No variation of this agreement or any of the documents in the agreed form shall be valid unless it is in writing and signed by or on behalf of each of the partie s to this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.6 No variation of this agreement or any of the documents in the agreed form shall be valid unless it is in writing and signed by or on behalf of either of the partie s to this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction by modifying the requirement for agreement variations. The original text requires that variations be 'signed by or on behalf of each of the parties,' implying unanimous consent. The altered text requires that variations be 'signed by or on behalf of either of the parties,' suggesting that a single party can unilaterally modify the agreement. This creates significant uncertainty about the process for amending the contract.This directly has an in-text contradiction with clause 9. ENTIRE AGREEMENT AND VARIATION, specifically regarding the entire agreement being followed.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 9. ENTIRE AGREEMENT AND VARIATION 9.1 This agreement and the documents referred to or incorporated in it constitute the entire agreement between the parties relating to the subject matter of this agreement and supersedes and extinguishes any prior drafts, agreements, undertakings, representations, warranties and 5 arrangements of any nature whatsoever, whether or not in writing, between the parties in relation to the subject matter of this agree ment. 9.2 Each of the parties acknowledges and agrees that it has not entered into this agreement in reliance on any statement or representation of any person (whether a party to this agreement or not) other than as expressly incorporated in this agreement . 9.3 Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, each of the parties irrevocably and unconditionally waives any right or remedy it may have to claim damages and/or to rescind this agreement by reason of any misrepresentation (other than a fraudulent misrepresentation) having been made to it by any person (whether party to this agreement or not) and upon which it has relied in entering into this agreement. 9.4 Without prejudice to clause 5.4, each of the parties acknowledges and agrees that the only cause of action available to it under the terms of this agreement and the documents referred to or incorporated in this agreement shall be for breach of contract. 9.5 Nothing contained in this agreement or in any other document referred to or incorporated i n it shall be read or construed as excluding any liability or remedy as a result of fraud. 9.6 No variation of this agreement or any of the documents in the agreed form shall be valid unless it is in writing and signed by or on behalf of either of the partie s to this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text of clause 9.6 requires all parties to sign off on any variations to the agreement, ensuring unanimous consent. The changed text alters this requirement, stating that only one party needs to sign off on variations. This directly contradicts the principle of the entire agreement as outlined in clause 9.1, which emphasizes that the agreement represents the complete understanding between the parties and supersedes any prior arrangements. Therefore, by allowing unilateral modifications, the changed text introduces a strong in-text contradiction with the fundamental principle of mutual agreement.
